title: Origami paper hat
subtitle: Folding the traditional peaked cap from a single rectangular sheet
excerpt: The paper hat, a peaked cap folded from a single rectangle, is one of the oldest and most widely distributed folded paper forms. Made from a single A4 sheet it produces a small desktop ornament; made from a full broadsheet newspaper it produces a hat that fits an adult's head. The fold logic is identical at any scale, and the finished hat can also be opened from its base to form a small open-topped basket.
sourceNotes: The traditional paper hat fold is among the oldest documented paper-folding forms in Europe and is depicted in Dutch genre paintings from the seventeenth century. The fold sequence is documented in children's craft manuals from the nineteenth century onward and is considered traditional public-domain folk origami.

glossaryTerms.definition: An intermediate fold form produced on the way to the paper hat and other traditional models. A rectangle folded in half, with both top corners folded to the centre line and the bottom flaps folded up on each side, creating a pointed boat shape that can be pulled open from the base.
A fold in which the paper is folded toward you. The paper creates a valley shape along the fold line. In origami notation, shown as a dashed line.

The action of inserting fingers into a folded form and pulling the layers apart to create a three-dimensional shape from the flat folded state. The paper hat is opened from its base to become a wearable hat or a small basket.

content.text: made wearable. The same fold sequence, rectangle, diagonals folded in, brim turned up, is used in toy boats, party hats, and (at newspaper scale) a working gardener's sun hat. Learning the fold at A4 scale makes it easy to scale up.

items.name: Kami origami paper, 20 cm, produces a small decorative hat
items.substitutions: A full broadsheet newspaper sheet (approximately 600 × 750 mm) produces a hat that fits an adult head; A4 cartridge produces a small ornament or toy

content.text: the sheet in half lengthways (the longer axis). You now have a long rectangle, fold line at the top.
Fold the two top corners down to the centre line, both left and right corners meet at the middle of the folded-edge. You now have a pentagon shape with a pointed top.

Take the top layer of the bottom strip and fold it up along the base of the two diagonal folds, forming the brim. Crease firmly. Fold the brim up a second time (turning it over itself) for a double brim that stays up firmly.
Turn the hat over and repeat on the other side, fold the remaining bottom strip up to form the second brim, fold up twice.
by inserting both thumbs into the open base and gently pulling the front and back panels apart until the base is a square opening. The hat stands on a flat bottom and the peak is at the top.

The same hat form opened fully from the base (pulling all four sides outward rather than two) forms a small square basket or tray. Used at large newspaper scale, the opened form makes a useful temporary container for small objects or a waste-paper liner.

items.cause: The brims were not folded with sharp, firm creases.
items.fix: Re-fold from the start, creasing each brim fold firmly along its full width. Use a bone folder at A4 scale.

items.symptom: The two diagonal folds at the top do not meet in the centre
items.cause: The sheet was not folded exactly in half at the first step.
items.fix: Align edges carefully before creasing the first fold. Any misalignment at step 1 is magnified at each subsequent step.
